ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general soy milk entry (FOODON_03301740); no specific multigrain soy milk entry exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0EJX covers flavoured/sweetened soya milk; multigrain variant is not separately coded. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for multigrain soy milk packaged beverage.

main_ingredients: multigrain blend (oats, barley, brown rice, or similar grains)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single-serve carton of packaged multigrain soy milk is approximately 250 ml (250 g), commonly consumed as a breakfast or snack beverage in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Multigrain Soy Milk (Packaged) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Multigrain Soy Milk (Packaged), cr_id D02030003, with confidence 0.95. All per_100g values are taken verbatim from the HPB record.

gi_source.title: Soy milk, full-fat — GI value 34 (Atkinson et al., 2008; University of Sydney GI Database)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 34 is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for full-fat soy milk (Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC, Diabetes Care 2008). Multigrain soy milk is a soy-milk-based beverage with added grains; the soy milk GI of 34 is used as the closest defensible analogous estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (34 × 20.2) / 100 = 6.87, rounded to 6.8. Serving size 250g × 8.08g carbohydrate/100g = 20.2g carbohydrate per serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar (6.52g/100g) in a sweetened plant milk beverage; sodium is low at 53mg/100g but sugar-sweetened beverages independently raise blood pressure risk
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should choose unsweetened multigrain soy milk (sugar ≤0.5g/100g) to avoid the blood-pressure-raising effect of sugar-sweetened beverages, as supported by AHA 2018 guidance on SSB and hypertension risk; the low sodium content (53mg/100g) is not a concern but does not offset the sugar risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened plant milk (6.52g sugar/100g) with zero dietary fibre; liquid sugar is rapidly absorbed, causing acute postprandial glucose excursions
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this sweetened multigrain soy milk and substitute unsweetened soy milk (sugar <1g/100g), as the 6.52g/100g added sugar with zero dietary fibre constitutes a sugar-sweetened beverage that produces rapid glycaemic spikes without fibre-mediated attenuation, per ADA 2024 beverage guidance.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Added sugar load (6.52g/100g) in a beverage with zero dietary fibre; soy protein content (2.6g/100g) is a modest positive factor but insufficient to offset sugar risk at typical serving volumes
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, this sweetened multigrain soy milk should be consumed only occasionally or replaced with unsweetened soy milk; the soy protein (2.6g/100g) provides a minor cardioprotective benefit, but the 6.52g/100g added sugar in liquid form contributes to hypertriglyceridaemia and metabolic risk per ACC/AHA 2019 dietary guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar (6.52g/100g) with zero dietary fibre promotes pro-inflammatory pathways; soy isoflavones present but at insufficient concentration in diluted beverage form to qualify as anti-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should choose unsweetened soy milk to obtain soy isoflavone anti-inflammatory benefits without the pro-inflammatory added sugar load; this sweetened packaged form is not recommended due to the 6.52g/100g sugar with zero fibre.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Multigrain Soy Milk (Packaged) is a ready-to-drink plant-based beverage produced by blending soy milk with a multigrain blend — typically including oats, barley, brown rice, or similar whole grains — and sweetening with added sugar. It is sold in single-serve cartons and is widely available in Singapore supermarkets and convenience stores. The beverage has a mild, slightly sweet, nutty flavour with a smooth, thin consistency similar to conventional soy milk. Per 100g it provides 70 kcal, 2.6g protein, 2.99g fat, 8.08g carbohydrate (of which 6.52g is sugar), 0g dietary fibre, and 53mg sodium. The low sodium content (53mg/100g) is a positive attribute, but the 6.52g/100g added sugar with zero dietary fibre classifies this product as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) under clinical dietary guidelines. The glycaemic index is estimated at 34 (low GI, analogous to full-fat soy milk per University of Sydney GI Database), but the zero fibre content means liquid sugar is absorbed rapidly, producing acute postprandial glucose excursions. This product is not recommended for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or rheumatoid arthritis in its sweetened form; unsweetened multigrain soy milk is the preferred alternative for these populations. The soy protein content provides a modest cardioprotective benefit. The product is vegan, dairy-free, and halal-suitable, making it a popular breakfast and snack beverage across diverse communities in Singapore.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D02030003) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The food is a commercially packaged product with a canonical HPB entry. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using the closest approved analogous food (full-fat soy milk, GI 34), which is well-supported in the peer-reviewed literature.
notes: dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source (0.0g/100g reported); this is a processed packaged beverage where grain fibre is largely removed during processing. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific packaged multigrain soy milk product are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for D02030003 as of 2026. GI value of 34 is an analogous estimate based on full-fat soy milk from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008); no direct GI measurement for multigrain soy milk was found in the approved GI database. Glycaemic load per serving calculated as (34 × 20.2g carbohydrate per 250g serving) / 100 = 6.87, rounded to 6.8. This product is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) due to 6.52g sugar/100g with zero dietary fibre; it is not suitable for Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or rheumatoid arthritis management in its current sweetened form.
